pattern: Walk up to the square root of each element, counting and summing divisors; if exactly four exist, add their sum to the running total.
hints: How many divisors does a number n have if it equals p*q where p and q are distinct primes?
You only need to iterate up to sqrt(n) — each divisor d <= sqrt(n) pairs with n/d. Counting pairs lets you bail out early if the count already exceeds 4.
Accumulate divisor sum and count together in one pass up to sqrt(n). After the loop, if count is exactly 4, add the accumulated sum to your answer.

edge_inputs.description: single element with exactly 4 divisors (p*q form)
single perfect square with exactly 3 divisors — not 4
mixed: some with 4 divisors, some not
all elements have exactly 4 divisors, duplicates
single element equal to 1 — only 1 divisor
array of small primes — each has 2 divisors, result is 0
large element near constraint boundary with 4 divisors

stress.description: 10k elements drawn uniformly from [1, 100000] — exercises every divisor-count bucket
